2_ascii_long_truncating.doc: Wikipedia: React is a free and open-source front-end JavaScript library for building user interfaces based on UI components. It is maintained by Meta (formerly Facebook) and a community of individual developers and companies. React can be used to develop single-page, mobile, or server-rendered applications with frameworks like Next.js. Because React is only concerned with the user interface and rendering components to the DOM, React applications often rely on libraries for routing and other client-side functionality. React applications often rely on libraries for routing and other client-side functionality. A key advantage of React is that it only re-renders those parts of the page that have changed, avoiding unnecessary re-rendering of unchanged DOM elements. React was created by Jordan Walke, a software engineer working for Facebook, who released an early prototype of React called FaxJS. React was first deployed on Facebook's News Feed in 2011, and later on Instagram in 2012. It was open-sourced at JSConf US in May 2013. React Native, which enables native Android, iOS, and UWP development with React, was announced at Facebook's React Conf in February 2015 and open-sourced in March 2015. On April 18, 2017, Facebook announced React Fiber, a new set of internal algorithms for rendering, as opposed to React's old rendering algorithm, Stack. React Fiber was to become the foundation of any future improvements and feature development of the React library. The actual syntax for programming with React does not change; only the way that the syntax is executed has changed. React's old rendering system, Stack, was developed at a time when the focus of the system on dynamic change was not understood. Stack was slow to draw complex animation, for example, trying to accomplish all of it in one chunk. Fiber breaks down animation into segments that can be spread out over multiple frames. Likewise, the structure of a page can be broken into segments that may be maintained and updated separately. JavaScript functions and React Hooks make small, fast updates to React components possible. The original announcement was met with mostly positive reactions, with some commentators noting that it would force Facebook to be more transparent about its development process.

Python decorators are a powerful and flexible feature that allows developers to modify the behavior of functions or methods without changing their actual source code. At its core, a decorator is just a function that takes another function as an argument, adds some kind of functionality, and returns another function. This is done using the @decorator_name syntax, placed directly above the function definition. Common use cases for decorators include logging, enforcing access control, memoization, instrumentation, timing functions, and adding metadata. The decorator pattern is heavily used in web frameworks like Flask and Django for routing and authentication, in unit testing frameworks like pytest for test markers and fixtures, and in standard library modules like functools for caching with @lru_cache. The key to understanding decorators is recognizing that in Python, functions are first-class objects: they can be passed as arguments to other functions, assigned to variables, stored in data structures, and returned from other functions. A decorator leverages this by wrapping a function inside another function, intercepting calls to the wrapped function, and optionally modifying the input arguments, the output return value, or both. More advanced decorators can take their own arguments, in which case they become decorator factories — functions that return decorators. The @property decorator is a built-in example, used to define computed attributes on classes. The @staticmethod and @classmethod decorators are also commonly used to indicate that a method should be called on the class itself rather than on an instance. Writing well-behaved decorators requires using functools.wraps to preserve the metadata of the wrapped function, such as its name and docstring. Without this, debugging and introspection become difficult because the wrapped function appears to be the decorator's inner function instead.

The TCP three-way handshake is the procedure used to establish a connection between a client and a server in a TCP/IP network. It consists of three steps. First, the client sends a SYN (synchronize) packet to the server. This packet contains an initial sequence number, which is a random number used to identify the start of the connection. Second, the server responds with a SYN-ACK (synchronize-acknowledge) packet, which acknowledges the client's SYN by sending an acknowledgment number equal to the client's sequence number plus one, and also contains the server's own initial sequence number. Third, the client sends an ACK (acknowledge) packet back to the server, acknowledging the server's SYN by sending an acknowledgment number equal to the server's sequence number plus one. After this three-way handshake, the TCP connection is established, and data can be transmitted in both directions. The connection is full-duplex, meaning that data can flow in both directions simultaneously. The three-way handshake is necessary to ensure that both sides have allocated resources for the connection and agreed on initial sequence numbers, which are used to detect duplicate or out-of-order packets. Termination of a TCP connection uses a four-way handshake, where each side independently sends a FIN packet and waits for an ACK from the other side. This allows each side to close its direction of the connection separately, supporting half-closed connections where one side has finished sending data but the other is still transmitting.

Kubernetes uses a master-worker architecture. The control plane (formerly called the master) consists of several components that manage the cluster: the API server, which exposes the Kubernetes API and is the front-end for the control plane; etcd, a consistent and highly available key-value store used as Kubernetes' backing store for all cluster data; the scheduler, which watches for newly created pods with no assigned node and selects a node for them to run on; and the controller manager, which runs controller processes that regulate the state of the cluster. Worker nodes run the kubelet (which makes sure that containers are running in a pod), the kube-proxy (a network proxy that maintains network rules on nodes), and a container runtime such as containerd or CRI-O. Pods are the smallest deployable units in Kubernetes and represent a single instance of a running process in the cluster. A pod can contain one or more containers that share storage and network resources and a specification for how to run the containers. Deployments provide declarative updates for pods and ReplicaSets, allowing you to describe an application's lifecycle, such as which images to use, the number of pods, and the way to update them. Services define a logical set of pods and a policy by which to access them — sometimes called a microservice. The set of pods targeted by a Service is usually determined by a selector.

Git rebase and Git merge are two of the most commonly used commands for integrating changes from one branch into another, and they accomplish the same goal but in very different ways. Merge takes the contents of a source branch and integrates them with a target branch by creating a new merge commit. This commit has two parents, one from each branch, preserving the history of both branches exactly as they happened. The resulting history reflects the actual chronological sequence of work and is sometimes called a non-linear history because of the diamond-shaped pattern created by merge commits. Rebase, on the other hand, takes the commits from a source branch and replays them on top of the target branch, one by one. Each commit is essentially reapplied as if it had been authored directly on top of the target branch, creating an entirely new linear sequence of commits. The original commits from the source branch are discarded after the rebase completes (though they can still be recovered from the reflog). Rebase produces a cleaner, linear history that is easier to read and reason about, especially in long-lived branches with many small commits. However, rebase rewrites history, which can be dangerous if the branch has already been pushed and shared with other contributors. The general rule is to use merge for shared branches like main or develop, and to use rebase for personal branches before merging them, to keep the project history clean. Interactive rebase, invoked with git rebase -i, provides additional power: it allows commits to be reordered, squashed, fixed up, edited, or dropped entirely. This is particularly useful for cleaning up a feature branch's history before merging it back into the main branch.

SQL injection (SQLi) is a type of security vulnerability that occurs when an attacker is able to insert or manipulate SQL queries that are executed by a backend database. This typically happens when user-supplied input is incorporated into a SQL query without proper validation or escaping. Successful SQL injection attacks can result in unauthorized access to sensitive data such as passwords, credit card numbers, or personal information; modification or deletion of data in the database; execution of administrative operations on the database; and in some cases, complete compromise of the underlying database server. There are several different types of SQL injection attacks, including in-band SQLi (the most common, where the attacker uses the same channel to launch the attack and gather results), inferential or blind SQLi (where the attacker sends data payloads to the server and observes the response and behavior of the server to learn about its structure), and out-of-band SQLi (which relies on features of the database server to make DNS or HTTP requests to deliver data to an attacker). The most effective way to prevent SQL injection is to use parameterized queries (also called prepared statements), which separate the SQL command from the data. Most database libraries support this. Object-relational mapping (ORM) tools and stored procedures can also help. Input validation, escaping, and the principle of least privilege for database users provide additional layers of defense. Web application firewalls (WAFs) can detect and block common SQL injection patterns, though they should not be relied upon as the sole defense.

GraphQL federation is an architecture for composing multiple independent GraphQL services into a single unified API. It was introduced by Apollo in 2019 as an evolution of schema stitching, which had become unwieldy at scale. In a federated architecture, individual services (called subgraphs) each define a portion of the schema, and a gateway combines them into a single supergraph that clients query. The federation specification allows each subgraph to extend types defined in other subgraphs, enabling true distributed schema ownership. For example, one subgraph might own the User type and define basic fields like id, name, and email, while another subgraph extends User to add purchase history or recommendations. The gateway routes incoming queries to the appropriate subgraphs based on which fields are requested, then combines the results. This approach has several advantages: each team can own and deploy their subgraph independently; schemas evolve organically without coordination overhead; and clients see a single unified API regardless of how the underlying services are organized. Apollo Federation 2, released in 2022, added improvements such as a new composition algorithm, support for shared types and shared fields, and better directive handling. The specification has been adopted by other GraphQL server implementations and there is now an open GraphQL Federation specification maintained by the GraphOS team. Common challenges with federation include handling cross-subgraph queries efficiently, managing entity resolution, dealing with versioning across subgraphs, and providing good developer tooling for working with the unified schema.

The ptrace system call in Linux provides a means by which one process (the tracer) may observe and control the execution of another process (the tracee), and examine and change the tracee's memory and registers. It is primarily used to implement breakpoint debugging (as in gdb) and system call tracing (as in strace). The tracer first attaches to the tracee, after which the tracee is stopped on the next signal or system call entry. The tracer can then read or write the tracee's memory, read or write its registers, deliver signals to it, and resume its execution one instruction at a time, one system call at a time, or until the next signal. Despite its power, ptrace has significant limitations: it is x86/ARM-specific in many of its details, the API is notoriously quirky (the tracer must waitpid on the tracee after each request), and it has had several security CVEs over the years involving the ability of one process to ptrace another (PTRACE_ATTACH semantics changed several times to address these). Modern Linux uses the YAMA Linux Security Module to restrict who can attach to whom (the kernel.yama.ptrace_scope sysctl). Many container runtimes drop the CAP_SYS_PTRACE capability by default. Alternatives to ptrace include eBPF (which provides a much safer way to inspect kernel and userspace activity), perf (which uses performance counters and kernel tracepoints), and the newer process_vm_readv and process_vm_writev syscalls for reading/writing another process's memory without the full ptrace dance.

Rust's borrow checker is a key component of the language's compiler that enforces the rules of ownership and borrowing at compile time. The borrow checker prevents data races, use-after-free, and other memory safety bugs by analyzing the lifetime and ownership of references in the code. The fundamental rules enforced are: each value has a single owner; at any given time, you can have either one mutable reference or any number of immutable references to a value, but not both; references must always be valid (no dangling pointers). The borrow checker performs this analysis statically, before the code is run, which means that programs that violate these rules simply will not compile. This approach trades a steeper initial learning curve for the guarantee that a large class of bugs are caught before deployment. Lifetime elision rules allow the compiler to infer lifetimes in many common cases, so explicit lifetime annotations are not always required. Non-lexical lifetimes (NLL), introduced in Rust 2018, made the borrow checker smarter by tracking the actual usage of references rather than their lexical scope, allowing patterns that previously required workarounds to compile naturally. The Polonius borrow checker, currently being developed, will further improve the precision of borrow checking. Common patterns for working with the borrow checker include using ownership transfer (move semantics), cloning when references would be too restrictive, and using interior mutability primitives like Cell, RefCell, Mutex, and RwLock when the borrow checker's compile-time rules would be too strict for the desired runtime behavior. The smart pointer types Box, Rc, and Arc provide heap allocation, shared ownership, and atomically-counted shared ownership respectively.

The Raft consensus algorithm is a distributed protocol used to manage a replicated log across a cluster of servers. It was designed as an alternative to Paxos, with the explicit goal of being more understandable. Raft achieves consensus through an elected leader: at any given time, one server is designated as the leader, and all other servers are either followers or candidates. The leader is responsible for accepting client requests, replicating log entries to the followers, and telling followers when to apply those entries to their state machines. If a follower fails to receive a heartbeat from the leader within a randomized timeout, it becomes a candidate, increments its term number, and requests votes from other servers. A candidate that receives a majority of votes for its term becomes the new leader. This approach ensures safety (only one leader per term) and liveness (eventually a leader is elected, provided that the network and a majority of servers are healthy). Raft separates the consensus problem into three relatively independent subproblems: leader election, log replication, and safety. Log entries flow only from the leader to followers, and the leader uses an AppendEntries RPC for both heartbeats and replication. Each entry is committed only when it has been replicated to a majority of servers, at which point the leader applies it to its state machine and tells followers to do the same. Configuration changes (adding or removing servers from the cluster) use a two-phase joint consensus approach to safely transition between configurations without violating the single-leader invariant. Many production systems use Raft, including etcd, Consul, CockroachDB, TiKV, and others. The Raft paper by Diego Ongaro and John Ousterhout is widely regarded as one of the most readable distributed systems papers.

4_emoji_zwj.doc: The family 👨‍👩‍👧 leave policy provides up to 12 weeks of paid time off for new parents.
We launched the 🚀 new feature 🎉 with a green deployment 🟢 yesterday.

5_special_tokens_in_text.doc: [SEP] is a special separator token used in BERT to separate two sentences in pair encoding.
The [CLS] token is prepended to every input sequence in BERT and its hidden state is used for classification.
RoBERTa uses <s> as the beginning-of-sequence token and </s> as the end-of-sequence and separator token.
The [PAD] token is used to pad sequences to a fixed length so they can be batched together.
The [UNK] token represents any token not in the vocabulary; rare in BPE but common in WordPiece.
GPT models use <|endoftext|> as the end-of-text marker between documents in training.
BERT's pretraining uses [MASK] to randomly replace 15% of tokens for masked language modeling.
The <s> tag in HTML represents text that is no longer accurate or relevant — strike-through.
When the input text contains [CLS][SEP] literally without spaces, tokenization differs from special-token handling.
Some tokenizers map <pad> to [PAD]; others treat <pad> as a literal text token if not registered.
